title: The rule of law in the besieged China Sea
primary_topics: rule of law vs rule of force
sub_topics: Japanese WWII occupation of Paracels and Spratlys
Mao's 1950 takeover of Paracels and Spratlys
North Vietnam's relinquishment in exchange for Vietnam War aid
current SCS occupation counts (Vietnam 29, PH 11, China 7, Malaysia 6, Taiwan 2, Brunei 1)
Taiwan recovery as Chinese internal affair vs self-determination
grey zone tactics (water cannons, lane cuttings)
Marcos pivot away from China toward US

stances.claim: China is constricted despite being a superpower — surrounded by hostile neighbors with only North Korea, Cambodia, and Mongolia as dependable allies; it needs the SCS to control sea lanes for its economy, to defend against US militarism, and to provide submarine passage to the Pacific and Indian oceans.
stances.rhetorical_move: structural-geopolitical analysis of Chinese strategic compulsion

stances.claim: The Philippines is 'an international pygmy in the rule of force but a giant in the rule of law' — the only country with an Arbitral Award to advance its sovereign rights in the SCS.
stances.rhetorical_move: asymmetric self-characterization + boast on unique legal asset

stances.claim: The Duterte administration tried to befriend and placate China to no avail — the Marcos regime correctly pivoted to the US for help and now exposes Chinese grey-zone tactics through open media coverage to the international discomfiture of China.

To insist on the rule of law is the only recourse of the Philippines to counter the rule of force in the China Sea — but China's selective rule-of-law invocation (claiming Taiwan as internal affair, ignoring AA on maritime rights) tests whether the rule of law is universally honored or only invoked when convenient.
stances.rhetorical_move: closing rhetorical question testing consistency of adversary's rule-of-law claim

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)
events: World War II Japanese occupation of Paracels and Spratlys
1945 Kuomintang acceptance of Japanese surrender in Taiwan/SCS
1950 Mao takeover of Paracels and Spratlys
1974 Chinese expulsion of South Vietnamese from Shanhu

signature_phrases: an international pygmy in the rule of force but a giant in the rule of law
to the Chinese, losing face is worse than losing life
to the international discomfiture of China
register_markers: CHINA AVOWS A LONG AND GLORIOUS HISTORY
CHINA HAS BECOME THE SECOND LARGEST ECONOMY
OUR CLAIM IN THE SCS IS BUTTRESSED
Will it also observe? (closing rhetorical question)
decision_framework_signals: applies rule-of-law-vs-rule-of-force binary to SCS geopolitics
uses structural-geopolitical analysis (constricted geography, hostile neighbors) to explain Chinese aggression as necessity-driven
uses asymmetric-asset framing — the AA is the Philippines' unique legal weapon
tests adversary's consistency on rule-of-law claims (Taiwan vs SCS) as moral/argumentative leverage
